Of course, commodity laws weren't designed for crypto assets; they were designed for assets like oil, gas, gold, and silver, which aren't based on centralized issuers or companies. Many crypto assets, while some are certainly affiliated with companies, mostly operate in decentralized environments where you trust the code and the network's operation. Therefore, providing this clarity is crucial. We also provide clarity regarding the listing and sale of new crypto assets. Sometimes the sale itself is subject to securities laws because you are selling certain promises and guarantees along with the asset. But once the asset begins trading on the market and these promises are no longer attached to it, the asset itself can be traded on the commodity market without the disclosure obligations under securities laws. This is very important for providing liquidity to the asset class and certainty for the American people.

V. Protecting Self-Custodial Rights and Software Developers

Host: You recently provided guidance for software developers. You mentioned the "no-action letter" from introducing brokers in your opening remarks. This is fundamentally about protecting developers. I know this is a particular concern for you, but how do we create an environment in the U.S. that ensures software developers can build here with confidence while maintaining this environment that protects self-custodial and non-custodial services? Mike Selig: Yes, **self-custody and software development in the United States must be protected.** Our nation is built on the idea of ​​private property. We must prevent governments or anyone else from seizing someone's crypto assets, depriving them of banking services, or cutting off their access to the financial system. Therefore, creating a safe harbor and clarity for software developers and providers of various types of self-custody software applications must be a priority for us as regulators in the United States. So, one of the first things the committee did after I took office was to issue a new position of action on software services in the self-custody wallet space. Typically, to access our derivatives markets, you go through a broker, who connects to an exchange, and it's all done in custody. You put in capital, you put in margin, and you trade in the derivatives market in custody. We've never had the ability to do it in a self-custody way, where you hold your own assets and transfer those assets to an exchange based on your trading activity, with margin moving between your wallet and the exchange. Now we have this new system: If you are a software developer providing a self-custodial wallet product, as long as the software development company meets certain criteria, the product and the software developer do not have to register with the commission. This will allow the use of Bitcoin and other crypto assets as collateral in our derivatives markets. You will be able to self-custody these assets, unlike some of our current markets where you use custodial wallet providers tied to exchanges. Here, you can use your own self-custodial wallet. We believe this will really help demonstrate the use cases for cryptocurrencies and blockchain because the core idea has always been that you can hold your own assets and rely on on-chain technology—trusting the code rather than your intermediaries.

VI. Why Market Structure Legislation Matters Now

Host: This is the regulatory level. And in Washington, all eyes are on the Clarity Act at the legislative level. I think this is a very complex topic. But one thing people should note is that it establishes strong regulation of intermediaries while also protecting the right of individuals to self-custody their assets. It protects software developers, and in practice, it should be able to foster industry growth. The Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) plays a very important role in this. Could you talk about how you see the Clearness Act's role in unlocking the CFTC's influence on Bitcoin and other crypto markets? Mike Selig: Well, as I said at the beginning, I do believe that the U.S. is the world's cryptocurrency capital today thanks to President Trump's leadership, but we must maintain that position. It's crucial to enact legislation to protect software developers, to protect this asset class, and to set clear rules. Because if we don't seize this opportunity to build some real legal safeguards, much of what we, as regulators, do could be overturned by the next administration. You'll see someone like Gary Gensler go back to the SEC or the CFTC, and he can overturn everything we've done, which is a real threat to this asset class. Of course, this asset class can operate outside of government agencies; it's designed to operate based on code, etc. But the reality is, governments have enormous power; they can really weaponize law enforcement and regulatory agencies to target people in this industry. We cannot allow this to happen again. I believe the best way to protect this asset class from future changes in the United States is to enact legislation that clearly declares crypto assets to be legitimate technology that can be used by ordinary Americans in business and finance. This would truly lay the foundation for the flourishing of this asset class in the United States. VII. Federal Clarity vs. 54 State Licensing Systems Host: The Clarity Act elevates the regulation of intermediaries from the state level—currently there are 54 different licensing jurisdictions (if you want to operate throughout the United States and its territories)—and concentrates most of the power in the Commodity Futures Trading Commission. I think this is a very welcome outcome for market participants. Mike Selig: Exactly. If you think about the historical trajectory of crypto assets, this is absolutely crucial. It all started with state systems. You have New York's BitLicense, and you have all those states' money transfer laws applied to cryptocurrency exchanges. Therefore, if they want to launch an exchange or custodial wallet, and even some non-custodial software providers have encountered trouble with various states, they have to cater to 50 different states and figure out their obligations. And now we're seeing that as the federal government adopts a more favorable system for cryptocurrencies, states are turning around and suing many exchanges. We just saw New York file lawsuits against two of the largest cryptocurrency exchanges in the US. They're essentially continuing the legal battle where Gary Gensler and the SEC stopped. So, establishing a federal system that protects you, that you have a federal license, and that states have no power to regulate these exchanges and assets after the fact, will be very important for the operation of the asset class in the United States. Mike Selig: Yes, it's very important to engage. We want to understand what you're building, what you're doing, the frictions you're experiencing in your business, and how we can help. In the past, as I said, you came in and got a subpoena, and the government tried to use the full force of the law against you. That's not what we're trying to do here. I've formed an Innovation Task Force, and we have leaders from industry, from the private sector, who go inside the government to look at these issues from a policy perspective. We've also drawn some of the best and brightest people within our agencies to help solve these problems. So we're inviting you to meet with the Task Force, tell us what you're doing, and let us find a way to help your business establish itself in the United States long-term. Moderator: Mr. Chairman, thank you for your public service and principled leadership. It's an honor to sit with you today and to see the progress you're making in Washington. Mike Selig: Yes, thank you. It's nice to be here.

data.title: ZetaChain Pauses Mainnet Cross-Chain Transactions After Attack On GatewayEVM Contract Sparks Fresh Bridge Security Fears
data.description:
Cross-chain activity on ZetaChain came to a sudden halt after a security incident hit its GatewayEVM contract, forcing the network to pause transfers while teams worked to contain the issue and assess potential exposure.

Cross-Chain Pause After Contract Attack Raised Alarm

ZetaChain confirmed that an attack targeted its GatewayEVM contract, prompting an immediate suspension of cross-chain transactions on its mainnet.

The team said the issue affected internal wallets only, not user deposits or funds.

In a public update on X, it stated:

“There was an attack against the ZetaChain GatewayEVM contract today that impacted the internal ZetaChain team wallets only. We’ve already blocked the attack vector so no more funds can be compromised and will be releasing a detailed post mortem after we have completed our investigation.”

As a precaution, the network paused cross-chain activity while engineers moved to patch the vulnerability and review the system.

There was an attack against the ZetaChain GatewayEVM contract today that impacted the internal ZetaChain team wallets only. We've already blocked the attack vector so no more funds can be compromised and will be releasing a detailed post mortem after we have completed our…

— ZetaChain 🟩 (@ZetaChain) April 27, 2026

Internal Wallets Hit But Were Users Exposed?

ZetaChain maintained that no user funds were impacted during the incident, despite early social media warnings suggesting a broader exploit.

The project added:

“As a precaution cross-chain transactions are currently paused on ZetaChain. Investigation is still ongoing and at this time no user funds were impacted by this attack.”

However, uncertainty spread quickly after a warning circulated on crypto channels claiming the bridge had been exploited, triggering calls for users to move assets.

The lack of an immediate full breakdown added to market concern, a common pattern in bridge-related incidents where information often lags behind speculation.

What Is The GatewayEVM Contract?

The compromised component, GatewayEVM, acts as the entry point for cross-chain activity between ZetaChain and external EVM-compatible networks such as Ethereum and Polygon.

It works alongside other core components including ERC20Custody, which holds tokens bridged into the network, and ZetaConnector, which manages token flows tied to ZETA across connected chains.

In simple terms, it is one of the main pathways used when assets or messages move between blockchains, making it a high-value target in interoperability networks.

🚨. @ZetaChain has been exploited. Based on initial analysis, the following outlines the root cause.

Root Cause
The core vulnerability lies in the call function of ZetaChain's GatewayZEVM contract, which lacks both access control and input validation. This allows any arbitrary… https://t.co/U63DKIfgDZpic.twitter.com/WbAHdiciRc

— SlowMist (@SlowMist_Team) April 28, 2026

Network Status Shows Systems Running But Transfers Frozen

Despite the incident, ZetaChain’s infrastructure remained largely operational.

Its status page showed all 11 mainnet components and testnet systems running at full uptime.

Only cross-chain transactions were disabled, with the incident listed as “identified” and “ongoing” for several hours while engineers confirmed the attack vector and prepared a fix.

At the time of reporting, the pause had lasted around nine hours since detection, with the team indicating a patch was being prepared.

How Much Was Lost In The Attack?

While ZetaChain has not confirmed exact figures, blockchain data cited by DefiLlama estimated losses of around $300,000.

NEW: @ZetaChain has reportedly been exploited, with at least $300K drained so far. pic.twitter.com/KcAaB1iOqD

— SolanaFloor (@SolanaFloor) April 27, 2026

The project has not publicly validated this figure and has said a detailed post-mortem will follow once the investigation concludes.

ZETA, the network’s token, holds a market capitalisation of roughly $73.3 million, with about 1.36 billion tokens in circulation out of a total supply of 2.1 billion.

Why Bridge Security Is Under Pressure Again

The incident lands during a turbulent period for decentralised finance, with April 2026 already recording one of the highest levels of crypto-related exploits in recent years.

Earlier this month, the Kelp DAO cross-chain exploit drained about $292 million, an incident later linked by multiple cybersecurity firms to the Lazarus Group.

Kelp DAO lost about $294M in a @LayerZero_Core bridge exploit.$ZRO dropped from $2 to $1.4, and a whale holding a $ZRO long on #HyperLiquid got partially liquidated, losing $2.88M.

The whale is still holding the position with a floating loss of over $750K. Overall, the whale… https://t.co/psD7U02563pic.twitter.com/1k7O4LJprw

— Onchain Lens (@OnchainLens) April 19, 2026

That attack alone triggered wider stress across DeFi, including heavy exposure risks on lending platforms such as Aave.

In response, an industry coalition named “DeFi United” began raising funds to cover losses, with contributions from major ecosystem players and founders helping push recovery efforts past $160 million.

defiunited.eth is now open for contributions. All contributions are going towards DeFi United relief efforts to restore rsETH and safe DeFi.https://t.co/gdkGe5pi0ehttps://t.co/lOlsI3SmVH

— Stani (@StaniKulechov) April 24, 2026

Across the month, more than $606 million has been lost across 12 separate incidents, according to DefiLlama data, with cross-chain bridges continuing to represent one of the most targeted attack surfaces in the sector.

Cross-Chain Design Remains A Persistent Weak Point

This latest ZetaChain incident adds to a long list of bridge and interoperability exploits in crypto history, including Ronin, Wormhole, and Nomad, each of which resulted in large-scale losses.

The repeated pattern has kept pressure on developers to strengthen verification systems, improve audit standards, and introduce safer withdrawal and transfer mechanisms for cross-chain liquidity.

ZetaChain has said it will publish a full post-mortem once its investigation is complete, while cross-chain services remain paused pending final confirmation of system integrity.
